claims.stance_label: Balanced governance with ethics and accountability
claims.citation: Deregulatory policies could erode accountability mechanisms, increasing the likelihood of deploying high-risk systems without adequate safeguards. Additionally, prioritizing private-sector autonomy over public safeguards may widen societal vulnerabilities and diminish trust in AI systems.

claims.stance_label: Support for safety standards and risk frameworks
claims.citation: Accel AI Institute is a proud member of the National AI Safety Institute Consortium (AISIC). Accel AI Institute contributes to the Consortium's efforts along with over two hundred stakeholders through strategic advice, resource sharing, and consulting on ethical AI development for all members.

claims.stance_label: Call for implementation of existing principles into law
claims.citation: This article is a call to action to refocus priorities, pull back from sci-fi level fears of AI, and understand what is already in place for AI regulation, but not being implemented. How do we get that to happen?

claims.stance_label: Risks real but manageable through responsible development
claims.citation: Artificial Intelligence (AI) is evolving to become one of the most important and most consequential key technologies of our time... AI holds great potential for economic growth as well as individual and societal well-being, but also comes along with risks for human rights, fundamental freedoms, democracy, and the environment.

claims.stance_label: Risks require research and mitigation, not prohibition
claims.citation: Viele weitere Potenziale von KI, aber auch die Risiken und wie wir damit umgehen, können nur durch zusätzliche Forschung erkannt werden.
Dem BMDS war in dem Prozess wichtig, dass das Gesetz ohne zusätzliche nationale Vorgaben und so schlank wie möglich umgesetzt wird, um Innovationen zu fördern und den Wirtschaftsstandort zu stärken.

claims.stance_label: Bias and unfair decision-making are key risks requiring mitigation
claims.citation: We are alert to the inherent risk of training AI on biased datasets, which can lead to biased outcomes and unfair decision-making. We seek to detect unfair bias and minimise its presence in our use of AI.

The accessibility and increasing sophistication of generative AI brings additional financial crime risks. While there is potential for the technology to support financial crime detection, there is also a risk that criminals use generative AI to perpetrate fraud, particularly scams.

HSBC continues to collaborate with regulators, partners, and industry peers to ensure technology is applied responsibly in fighting fraud. By combining human expertise with advanced AI, we are building a more secure and resilient banking experience for all.
We adapt existing governance and approval processes to address the ethical and technical implications of using data and AI. We escalate and address concerns to appropriate accountable executives.

claims.stance_label: Work with regulators on responsible AI
claims.citation: Because of the rapid pace of this technological evolution, we have to make sure that we're working with a range of partners and regulators to make sure that they're comfortable and understand what our strategic ambitions are.

We are committed to the responsible use of AI, ensuring that our initiatives align with industry and regulatory standards and best practices. Our governance frameworks aim to enable robust prioritisation of use cases whilst mitigating potential risk associated with AI deployment.

claims.stance_label: Existential risk out of scope; focus on documented harms
claims.citation: This paper is concerned with risks that have already been documented or directly follow from current methods. Impacts on future generations, such as existential risk, are out of scope.

ICONIQ's actions strongly suggest a light-touch regulatory stance: the firm has aggressively poured billions into frontier AI labs like Anthropic and OpenAI, facilitated access to global capital including Gulf sovereign wealth funds, and helped Anthropic navigate geopolitical concerns about international funding. There are no public statements from ICONIQ supporting significant AI regulation; their investment thesis frames the AI boom as an enduring opportunity rather than a risk requiring governance.

claims.definition_used: AGI (or machine superintelligence) is the outcome of the AI scaling strategy — superintelligent LLMs that can raise productivity and generate exponential output growth.
claims.citation: The AI industry is betting the U.S. economy on its strategy of 'scaling', i.e., adding more and more data, GPUs, compute infrastructure and dollars, in the belief that this will lead to machine superintelligence or Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) — and, of course, that the capital expenditures can be recouped, with a good rate of return, because the superintelligent Large Language Models (LLMs) will raise productivity, speed up technological progress and innovation, generating exponential growth of output as well as profits for their owners and the larger American economy.

claims.definition_used: AGI is defined as the ability to perform any cognitive task that humans can perform.
claims.citation: Yet the stated mission of several leading AI labs, including OpenAI, is even grander: that a future version of their foundation models will achieve artificial general intelligence (AGI), defined as the ability to perform any cognitive task that humans can perform.

claims.stance_label: AGI via scaling will remain a dream
claims.citation: My main point is simple: the 'scaling' strategy to achieve AGI is already showing significant diminishing returns, because more data and more GPUs cannot fundamentally improve the performance of LLMs, after a point. The algorithms are not constructed on proper and robust world models, but instead are built to autocomplete, based on sophisticated pattern-matching. As a result, AGI will remain a dream and the magical projections of exponential growth, which defy economic and financial logic and fatally ignore unforgiving physical (analogue) constraints imposed by the electricity grid, the construction industry, and the shortage of (skilled) workers who are needed to help build the additional data center infrastructure, will turn out to be wrong.

claims.stance_label: Serious risks from concentration and safety failures
claims.citation: AI experts warn that future more capable AI systems could cause wide-ranging and perhaps even irreversible harm to society (Hinton et al 2023; Anderljung et al. 2023). These concerns include the potential malfunctioning of powerful AI systems as well as the malicious use of such AI systems. The further advancement of AI could potentially give rise to many problems, including drastic misuse by unscrupulous actors, catastrophes arising from errors in understanding human preferences, or even human disempowerment or extinction (Hendrycks et al. 2022).

We develop a theoretical framework to identify the properties that make an innovation desirable from the perspective of workers, including its technological complementarity to labor, the factor share of labor in producing the goods involved, and the relative income of the affected workers.

The social issues raised by AI, including questions of fairness, privacy, value alignment, accountability, and automation, can only be resolved through democratic control of algorithm objectives, and of the means to obtain them - data and computational infrastructure. Democratic control requires public debate and binding collective decision-making, at many different levels of society.

In the short term, policymakers must implement proactive competition policies to address these concerns. These could include mandating data sharing to mitigate the effects of data feedback loops, promoting interoperability and common API standards to reduce switching costs, and encouraging more sharing of research results to counteract the concentration of capabilities.

claims.definition_used: AGI is a future state of powerful AI capable of reasoning and accomplishing larger goals in the world, where humans remain in control rather than being subordinated to the technology.
claims.citation: We founded Imbue in 2021 to build an AGI future where humans remain at the helm, shaping powerful AI systems rather than being subordinated to them.

claims.definition_used: AGI requires robust reasoning capability — the ability to deal with uncertainty, change approach, gather information, make decisions, and handle the complicated real world — which current AI systems lack.
claims.citation: We believe reasoning is the primary blocker to effective AI agents. Robust reasoning is necessary for effective action. It involves the ability to deal with uncertainty, to know when to change our approach, to ask questions and gather new information, to play out scenarios and make decisions, to make and discard hypotheses, and generally to deal with the complicated, hard-to-predict nature of the real world.
Submitted as "5-10 years" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Inferred from actions)

claims.stance_label: LLM ethical reasoning failures pose real risk
claims.citation: We are extremely excited about what can be built on top of LLMs, but today they're not suitable for cases where they must reason about moral situations. Significant work remains to be done to improve their robustness and our understanding of how they work (and where they fail).

claims.stance_label: Serious but manageable with proper policy
claims.citation: AI will make many tasks easier – including tasks that may cause serious damage and tasks that are motivated by malicious intent. Our safety opportunity with AI is to pursue a program that minimizes much of that harm both by supporting the development of safe systems and by preventing the deployment of unsafe ones.

claims.stance_label: Power concentration as serious societal risk
claims.citation: We've now come to understand something crucial: the core challenge of AI lies in managing how it shifts power. AI makes software systems dramatically more powerful. That power flows, by default, to those who can build and own those systems. This concentrates power, which leads to exploitation and disempowerment.
For a regulation to have force, it will need not only financial penalties, but the ability to shut down non-compliant systems and to hold irresponsible actors to account.

Corporations were very likely to recommend 'risk-based' regulation as the optimal approach for managing AI's concerns. This isn't surprising – risk regulation focuses on impact assessments and internal risk mitigation at the expense of tort liability and individual rights, which certainly favors corporate interests.

claims.stance_label: Targeted interoperability rules, not broad regulation
claims.citation: The solution is to protect interoperability and access for AI agents, so that individuals can regain control over their data and digital lives without the worry of arbitrary restrictions. No overly burdensome regulation is needed to spur the startup ecosystem to unlock competing approaches to AI agents.

claims.stance_label: Policy to shape agentic AI ecosystem
claims.citation: Last week we joined Anthropic, Intuit, Edgerunner AI, Unstructured, Shift5, and Lilt AI to launch the Agentic Futures Initiative (AFI) to help shape clear, practical, and forward-looking policy for AI agents.

Inclusive Abundance Action promotes an 'abundance agenda' that explicitly frames scarcity-oriented regulation as an obstacle to progress. Its lobbying covers AI under a science/technology issue area alongside energy permitting and housing deregulation, suggesting a preference for enabling innovation over restrictive governance. Its funding of ARI, which advocates for 'thoughtful governance' that 'fosters innovation,' is consistent with a light-touch or moderate stance rather than a restrictive one.

claims.definition_used: AGI is an all-knowing and all-capable machine that thinks like a human.
claims.citation: But at the time, a lot of companies were talking about artificial general intelligence, or AGI, and the idea of creating an all-knowing and all-capable machine that thinks like a human.

AI will present similar problems. But I'm confident that we'll figure them out, and on balance the trajectory will be extraordinarily positive for people. I tend to share the optimistic viewpoint that it will make our lives better in the long term, though there will certainly be turbulence in that process.

At a policy level, are there rules needed around synthetic datasets? Historical data sets that we have are potentially concentrated on certain populations or certain countries, so would you introduce synthetic data to feed to the algorithm to correct for historical bias?

claims.stance_label: Superlearner — knowledge discovery beyond human data
claims.definition_used: A 'superlearner' is an AI system that can develop skills and discover knowledge without relying on human-generated data as its primary input, discovering solutions through experience rather than pattern-matching on existing human knowledge.
claims.citation: The company's goal is to build what it calls a 'superlearner,' a system that can develop skills and discover knowledge without relying on human-generated data as its primary input. That vision, if it works, could push AI beyond pattern recognition into something more open-ended, where systems generate their own paths to solutions.

claims.definition_used: Superintelligence (used as their operative target) means systems capable of independently discovering breakthroughs in science, technology, government, economics, and other domains — described by Silver as 'making first contact with superintelligence.'
claims.citation: Silver describes the company's mission as 'making first contact with superintelligence.' By superintelligence, he means systems capable of independently discovering breakthroughs in science, technology, government, economics, and other domains.

claims.definition_used: AGI (strong AI) is a machine that performs in human-like ways, learns and aims to replicate human understanding and problem-solving, and potentially develops a human-like consciousness; it is theoretical at present and unlikely to be developed soon.
claims.citation: In contrast, strong AI, or artificial general intelligence, refers to a machine that performs in human-like ways. It learns and aims to replicate human understanding and problem-solving by learning and potentially teaching itself to develop a human-like consciousness instead of imitating it. It is theoretical at present and unlikely to be developed soon.

claims.stance_label: AGI is theoretical and not imminent
claims.citation: In contrast, strong AI, or artificial general intelligence, refers to a machine that performs in human-like ways... It is theoretical at present and unlikely to be developed soon. Critics emphasize the need for caution in developing strong AI.

claims.stance_label: Risks are real but manageable with guardrails
claims.citation: People have accepted that, like any other general-purpose technology be it electricity, nuclear energy, the internet or even a discovery like fire, gen AI has enormous potential for good when explored and advanced within the guardrails of responsibility.

claims.stance_label: AI risks addressed through frameworks and standards
claims.citation: The framework aims to monitor and protect AI models and systems from risks and threats, while enabling businesses to apply AI responsibly. As part of the consortium, Infosys will bring to the table its deep expertise, capabilities, and point of views to support safe development and deployment of generative AI.

The initial hyperventilation of AI doomerism and the risk of human extinction by AI advances like Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) has quietened down... many of the doomsday prophets pleading for extensive AI regulation have revealed themselves to be just protectionists who want to limit the fruits of gen AI to a few companies and investors.

As AI becomes central to driving enterprise growth, its ethical adoption is no longer optional. The Infosys Responsible AI Toolkit ensures that businesses remain resilient and trustworthy while navigating the AI revolution. By making the toolkit open source, we are fostering a collaborative ecosystem that addresses the complex challenges of AI bias, opacity, and security.

Infosys has been selected as an inaugural member of the AI Safety Institute Consortium, created by the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST), an agency of the United States Department of Commerce, to jointly develop guidelines and standards for AI security and Responsible AI adoption along with other members of the consortium.

claims.definition_used: AGI is a theoretical form of advanced AI that would have capabilities that compare to or exceed humans across most economically valuable work.
claims.citation: Artificial General Intelligence (AGI): A theoretical form of advanced AI that would have capabilities that compare to or exceed humans across most economically valuable work. A number of AI companies have publicly stated their aim to build AGI and believe it may be achievable within the next twenty years. Other experts believe we may not build AGI for many decades, if ever.

The scheme launched today (Tuesday 15th October), in partnership with the Engineering and Physical Sciences Research Council (EPSRC) and Innovate UK, part of UK Research and Innovation (UKRI), is focused on how society can be protected from the potential risks of AI.

In October 2023, the CDEI and Innovate UK launched the 'Fairness Innovation Challenge', a grant challenge to drive the development of new sociotechnical solutions to address bias and discrimination in AI systems.
We are also working with Innovate UK and the Alan Turing Institute to develop guidance that sets out the core AI skills people need, from 'AI citizens' to 'AI professionals'.

claims.definition_used: AGI represents the highest level of agentic AI autonomy — hypothetical agents that exhibit original thinking and can synthesize solutions to previously unseen tasks.
claims.citation: Level 5 – Autonomy: Represents what many consider Artificial General Intelligence (AGI). These hypothetical agents exhibit original thinking and can synthesize solutions to previously unseen tasks.

While this has created tremendous opportunity for innovation, there has also been an increased concern around misuse, safety, bias and misinformation. We at Intel believe that responsible development must be the foundation of innovation throughout the AI life cycle to ensure AI is built, deployed and used in a safe, sustainable and ethical way.

Intel has long recognized the importance of the ethical and human rights implications associated with the development of technology. This is especially true with the development of AI technology, for which we remain committed to evolving best methods, principles, and tools to ensure responsible practices in our product use, development, and deployment, with the aim of lowering risks while optimizing benefits for our society.

Intel encourages AI policy measures that consider context and sector specific use cases and applications rather than horizontal requirements and urges that care be taken so that regulatory initiatives are limited to issues not already covered by existing laws, regulations, or international standards.

Intel advocates for risk-based, principles-driven, and interoperable AI policy and regulatory measures. Intel supports a risk-based, multi-stakeholder approach to trustworthy AI informed by international standards (e.g., ISO/IEC) and frameworks such as the National Institute for Standards and Technology (NIST) AI Risk Management Framework.

claims.citation: Because fundamentally, the reason we're in this weird position now where everyone is racing to build something that nobody actually believes is a good thing is the coordination failure.

claims.citation: While regulation is important, it is key that it does not put at risk the budding field of open-source Generative AI. We argue for the responsible open sourcing of generative AI models in the near and medium term.

claims.definition_used: AGI is the threshold at which AI systems can perform tasks as well as humans.
claims.citation: The Silicon Valley billionaire is committing $1 billion of his cryptocurrency fortune–worth about $3.9 billion by Forbes' estimate–into building AI systems that achieve artificial general intelligence, or the threshold at which AI systems can perform tasks as well as humans.

claims.stance_label: Transformers won't get there; needs new paradigm
claims.citation: When McCaleb and George first met, they bonded over their shared skepticism that scaling transformers – the AI architecture underpinning products like ChatGPT – will produce AGI. They think the field needs to go back to the drawing board.
Their method of open science, neuroscience integration, and creating a decade-long funding model is a purposeful alternative to the commercial AI labs' secretive and short-term-oriented approach.

claims.stance_label: Current AI opaque and risky; brain-inspired AI safer
claims.citation: McCaleb touts brain-inspired AI as a pathway to safer AI. Today's frontier models are gargantuan and opaque; understanding what they 'know' and why they give the answers they do remains difficult.

claims.stance_label: AI misalignment poses existential threat requiring safety measures
claims.citation: Jed McCaleb has made significant donations to the Machine Intelligence Research Institute (MIRI), an organization dedicated to AI alignment research aimed at mitigating existential risks from advanced artificial intelligence systems.

No explicit public statements on AI regulation found, but McCaleb's investment pattern—funding open science compute infrastructure (Voltage Park), supporting MIRI, donating to OpenAI, and endowing the Navigation Fund to accelerate 'bold scientific bets' that bypass 'protracted bureaucratic processes like multi-stage peer reviews'—strongly suggests a preference for minimizing bureaucratic friction and enabling rapid AI development with light regulatory oversight.

We want to make sure that these regulations actually make models safer, and that audits are grounded in (our) state-of-the-art interpretability work.

claims.stance_label: Risks must be anticipated and managed
claims.citation: "Like with any new technology, generative AI comes with risks that must be anticipated and managed. For instance, LLMs risk 'hallucinations'—falsehoods that are presented as facts—and inadvertently disclosing sensitive information present in the training data."

claims.entity_name: Johns Hopkins University Applied Physics Laboratory
claims.citation: "A 'Framework for Identifying Highly Consequential AI Use Cases' recognizes that AI can help improve health, education and productivity, as well as provide the data required to solve ongoing global problems. However, AI also has the potential to spread disinformation, promote discrimination and be deployed in cyberattacks."

Clymer writes about 'Planning for Extreme AI Risks' on Substack and his research focuses on alignment failures and out-of-distribution generalization problems, suggesting concern that AI development needs safety infrastructure before scaling. His work implies support for slowing or carefully managing AI development until alignment is better understood.
Submitted as "Unknown" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Inferred from associations)
Submitted as "Serious" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Inferred from associations)

Inferred from funding ARI, which advocates for a thoughtful governance framework for AI that protects the public from harm while fostering innovation, takes a bipartisan approach, and focuses on catastrophic risk prevention and compute governance without opposing AI development broadly.
Submitted as "Unknown" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Inferred from actions)

Conversely, as AI and GenAI usage within the enterprise new risks swiftly emerge from governance to cyberrisk. Enterprises are faced with the critical task of reassessing their existing security frameworks and adopting novel tools to protect essential AI system components, such as interfaces, data sources, inference APIs, outputs, and the models themselves.
By following these pathways, organizations can harness GenAI's benefits while proactively managing risks and ensuring compliance with relevant regulations and ethical standards.

Establishing AI governance frameworks that define roles, responsibilities, and policies ensures compliance with regulations and ethical guidelines. Ensuring transparency and explainability of AI models builds trust and accountability.

Now, our focus is on understanding how these developments affect AI model governance across different industries and create specific software requirements in verticals like financial services, manufacturing, industrials, and life sciences.
Submitted as "Unknown" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Inferred from actions)
Submitted as "Unknown" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Inferred from actions)

No explicit regulatory positions found in sources. His work focuses on AI safety community-building and model evaluations rather than policy advocacy, making his regulatory stance unclear.

claims.definition_used: AGI is an AI that can reason and answer new, previously unlearned questions — moving beyond memorization toward genuine understanding; it must be proficient across all fields, not just one specialized domain.
claims.citation: 'If it gives a reasonable answer when asked a question from the learned list, but cannot answer a question it has not learned, it cannot be called AGI. If we can reason and answer new questions, we can think of it as AGI.' ... 'AGI must be good at all fields, not just one specialized field.'

claims.definition_used: AGI requires the ability to systematically generalize from learned concepts to understand and imagine never-before-seen concepts — described as 'systematic generalization' or 'compositional generalization' — encompassing reasoning and imagination capabilities essential for general intelligence.
claims.citation: 안성진 KAIST 교수는 '시각 정보의 체계적 일반화가 AGI를 달성하기 위해 필수적인 능력'이라며 '이 연구를 통해 AI의 추론능력과 상상능력 관련 분야의 발전을 가속할 것으로 기대한다'고 말했다. [Translation: KAIST Professor Ahn Sung-jin stated that 'systematic generalization of visual information is an essential capability for achieving AGI' and that 'this research is expected to accelerate advances in AI reasoning and imagination capabilities.']

claims.stance_label: AGI concerns premature; standards not clearly defined
claims.citation: Professor Choi is the director of KAIST's XAI (Explainable Artificial Intelligence) Research Center... 'I think concerns that AGI will appear immediately are premature.' ... 'The standards for AGI are not clearly defined.'

claims.stance_label: AGI possible in 1-2 years (aggressive estimate from KAIST professor)
claims.citation: Lee Ki-min, a professor at the KAIST Kim Jae-chul Graduate School of AI, offered a more aggressive view, stating, 'It will be possible in at least one to two years.' Professor Lee also mentioned the possibility that companies like OpenAI are already preparing to release an AGI.

claims.stance_label: AI security risks are serious and require active research
claims.citation: Professors Seungwon Shin and Sooel Son stated, 'Through this study, we have empirically confirmed that the MoE structure, which is spreading rapidly for the sake of efficiency, can become a new security threat. This award is a meaningful achievement that recognizes the importance of AI security on an international level.'

claims.stance_label: Responsible AI with international governance focus
claims.citation: KAIST President Lee Kwanghyung said, 'Professor Kim's selection as a member of the AI Scientific Panel is a remarkable achievement that demonstrates the stature of South Korea's AI research beyond KAIST,' adding, 'KAIST will work with the international community to scientifically analyze the socio-economic ripple effects of AI and contribute to proposing policy directions for responsible AI development.'

Professor Jong-cheol Ye of KAIST (Director of the Promotion Council for Digital Health), in particular, emphasized that it is important to find a point of balance that does not hinder the advancements rather than opting to enforcing strict regulations.

claims.entity_name: Capital K (KAUST Deep Tech Innovation Fund)
claims.citation: Capital K and the broader KAUST strategy are oriented toward accelerating commercialization and deep tech investment with no stated regulatory constraints. The fund is designed to operate independently and flexibly, focused on growth and economic diversification rather than imposing governance requirements on AI development.

claims.stance_label: Ethical and social risks warrant study
claims.citation: The Kavli Centers for Ethics, Science, and the Public became fully focused on a proactive and sustained effort to connect scientists, ethicists, social scientists, science communicators, and publics to discuss potential impacts of scientific discoveries.

claims.stance_label: ELSI risks merit data-driven global study
claims.citation: Since 2020, Yokoyama's team has been carrying out AI ELSI research. Her group has already developed an AI ELSI score 'Octagon measurement' which measure the level of the ELSI and 'AI ELSI segment' which group people with similar opinions about ELSI through data-driven analysis based on data from several countries across Asia, the US and Europe.

One university funding effort comes from the Kavli Foundation, which in 2021 committed $1.5 million each over five years to two new centers focused on scientific ethics — with artificial intelligence as one priority area — at the University of California at Berkeley and the University of Cambridge.

The project will embark on data-driven studies that will bring Asian perspectives into the global discussions surrounding a subset of ethical, legal and social issues (ELSI) associated with Artificial Intelligence and Climate Engineering.

Submitted as "Unknown" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Inferred from actions)

As the use of AI applications has increased, the demand for unbiased, trustworthy and explainable AI has become paramount. We have been at the forefront of research in ethical AI, developing methods and leading studies in how AI can be trusted, how it can make moral decisions like humans, and how it can be harnessed to benefit society while minimizing risk.

Kitware is excited to participate in the NIST AI Safety Institute Consortium, and we plan to contribute our deep expertise and open source tools in AI system design, explainable AI, and AI test and evaluation. Kitware is committed to AI for social good.
Kitware Awarded $3.9M DARPA Contract to Develop Standards for Evaluating Ethics in AI-Driven Military Technologies.

claims.citation: Submitted as "5-10 years" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Inferred from actions)

claims.entity_name: LASR Labs (London AI Safety Research Labs)
claims.citation: London AI Safety Research (LASR) Labs is an AI safety research programme focussed on reducing the risk of loss of control to advanced AI. We focus on action-relevant questions tackling concrete threat models.

claims.entity_name: LASR Labs (London AI Safety Research Labs)
claims.citation: LASR Labs does not publish explicit regulatory positions, but its entire mission is premised on the urgency of reducing risks from advanced AI before loss of control occurs. The programme focuses on 'action-relevant questions tackling concrete threat models' and explicitly frames its work around preventing catastrophic outcomes, suggesting alignment with a precautionary worldview that AI development requires serious safety infrastructure now.

claims.definition_used: AGI as a system achievable through scaling compute alone, implying it involves general-purpose intelligence that matches or exceeds human cognitive capability across domains.
claims.citation: "This is the strongest evidence that I've seen so far that we will achieve AGI with just scaling compute. It's genuinely starting to concern me. I used to think that we would run into roadblocks: end of scaling laws, maybe we don't have the right model architecture, power density."

"Der entsprechende Referentenentwurf führt zu einer massiven Schwächung von Grundrechten", so Meike Kamp, Berliner Beauftragte für Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit. "Laut KI-Verordnung ist es originäre Aufgabe der Datenschutzaufsichtsbehörden, in diesem Bereich der Marktüberwachung die Einhaltung von Grundrechten zu kontrollieren."

claims.stance_label: Structured AI governance with transparency requirements
claims.citation: "Berlin muss seine Verwaltung fit machen, um KI-Technologien transparent und effektiv einzusetzen. Bündnis 90/Die Grünen fordern den Senat daher auf, eine ressortübergreifende KI-Governance zu etablieren, die sowohl auf etablierter KI-Ethik wie der des Deutschen Ethikrats aufbaut als auch Rechtssicherheit für 'First Mover' in Wirtschaft, Verwaltung, Wissenschaft und Zivilgesellschaft garantiert."

claims.stance_label: Proactive AI adoption with high data protection standards
claims.citation: "The system is therefore subject to high data protection and security standards. An integrated help center with video tutorials supports users in working with the AI. According to the Senate Chancellery, the state is responding to the shortage of skilled workers and demographic changes with BärGPT."

claims.stance_label: Value-based, human-centred AI with transparency and accountability
claims.citation: "Create value-based, human-centred AI systems for use in the public sector, strengthening its pioneering role in the research on secure and trustworthy technology design: Foster transparency and accountability i.e. by revealing when automated decision-making processes are used in digital public services, and ensure quality standards of data sets fed into AI systems when designing digital public services."
